message.content:
Do a general web search (not limited to official Anthropic docs) to find what these Claude Code diagnostic items mean. Search GitHub issues, blog posts, reverse-engineering writeups, Hacker News, Reddit, etc.

Items:
- "isGrowthBookEnabled", "hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures", "growthBookFeaturesLoaded", "growthBookLastFetched", "DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK" in Claude Code
- "tengu" as Claude Code's internal codename (e.g. tengu_ prefixed analytics events / feature flags)
- Specifically: what is "tengu_ccr_bridge"? Note that "CCR" in the Claude Code community often refers to "claude-code-router" (the musistudio/claude-code-router proxy project) — search whether tengu_ccr_bridge relates to that, or to something else (e.g. "Claude Code Remote", "Claude Code on the web"). Try searches like: "tengu_ccr_bridge", "claude code" "ccr_bridge", "claude code" growthbook feature flags list, "tengu_" claude code statsig growthbook.
- Also check whether Claude Code recently migrated from Statsig to GrowthBook for feature flags/telemetry.

Report what you find with sources (URLs), clearly separating confirmed facts from speculation. Be concise.

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

How DISABLE_TELEMETRY Caused the Issue","url":"https://zenn.dev/m0370/articles/d7e77adebd0ba8?locale=en"},{"title":"Bug: `/remote-control` intermittently unavailable — `tengu_ccr_bridge` flag flips to `false` after background GrowthBook cache refresh · Issue #34267 · anthropics/claude-code","url":"https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/34267"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about `tengu_ccr_bridge`:

## Overview

`tengu_ccr_bridge` is a server-side GrowthBook feature flag that gates the Remote Control feature in Claude Code, defaulting to false. Tengu is presumably Claude Code's internal project codename at Anthropic.

## What It Controls

The tengu_ccr_bridge flag controls the Remote Control feature that allows controlling the local machine's Claude Code from the Claude.ai web interface. The flag enforces a minimum Claude Code version for Remote Control bridge usage and prompts users to update when their version is too old.

## Key Characteristics

- The flag is evaluated server-side and cannot be overridden locally
- The GrowthBook gate check for tengu_ccr_bridge is performed by the CCR (Claude Cloud Runtime) API as part of the subscription validation process

## Known Issues

Multiple users have reported issues where the flag returns `false` even for Max plan subscribers who should have access. Some users have found that logging out and back in refreshes the cached feature flag and unlocks it. The flag may also go missing from cache if telemetry updates are disabled, since Remote Control was released in February 2025.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: ""claude code" "DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK" growthbook feature flags"

Links: [{"title":"DISABLE_TELEMETRY silently disables GrowthBook — which also gates preview features (Agent View) and remote killswitches · Issue #58383 · anthropics/claude-code","url":"https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/58383"},{"title":"CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC blocks channels feature flag (tengu_harbor) · Issue #45918 · anthropics/claude-code","url":"https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/45918"},{"title":"GrowthBook - Claude Wiki","url":"https://claude-wiki.com/growthbook.html"},{"title":"growthbook/CLAUDE.md at main · growthbook/growthbook","url":"https://github.com/growthbook/growthbook/blob/main/CLAUDE.md"},{"title":"Telemetry opt-out should not disable Channels feature flag · Issue #38450 · anthropics/claude-code","url":"https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/38450"},{"title":"330 Env Variables: Claude Code Full Config Exposed | ThePlanetTools.ai","url":"https://theplanettools.ai/blog/claude-code-330-env-variables-32-feature-flags"},{"title":"Feature Flags and Growth | claude-code-best/claude-code | DeepWiki","url":"https://deepwiki.com/claude-code-best/claude-code/13.2-feature-flags-and-growth"},{"title":"Lack of transparency and user consent for experimental features and telemetry · Issue #25141 · anthropics/claude-code","url":"https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/25141"},{"title":"Feature Flag Platform - Open Source | GrowthBook","url":"https://www.growthbook.io/products/feature-flags"},{"title":"CLAUDE_CODE_WORKFLOWS env var silently gates the workflows feature even when tengu_workflows_enabled is true (same pattern as #50083) · Issue #61825 · anthropics/claude-code","url":"https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/61825"}]

Here are the search results for "claude code" "DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK" and GrowthBook feature flags:

## Key Findings

**DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK Environment Variable**

DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK serves as an explicit "no flag traffic, accept frozen defaults" escape hatch. When GrowthBook is suppressed, a --debug note should surface, and/or a hint on the claude agents fallback explaining "Agent View unavailable — feature flags disabled by DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK".

**GrowthBook's Role in Claude Code**

GrowthBook is the remote feature flag management system used by Claude Code to control runtime behavior without requiring users to update their installation. The CLI polls the GrowthBook API every hour, receiving a JSON payload of flag values that can alter behavior, enable or disable features, and act as remote kill switches.

**Related Issues**

A significant issue exists where setting DISABLE_TELEMETRY=1 (or DO_NOT_TRACK) puts Claude Code into a "no-telemetry" traffic mode that disables GrowthBook entirely. GrowthBook isn't only experiment-exposure logging — it's also the delivery mechanism for feature flags, which gate preview features (e.g. Agent View / claude agents / --bg / /bg) and remote killswitches. This creates an unintended consequence where users who disable telemetry for privacy also lose access to gated features.

**Feature Flags Available**

Claude Code uses 22+ GrowthBook runtime feature gates prefixed with "tengu_", which enable A/B testing and gradual rollout of features for specific user segments.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about "Tengu" and its relationship to Claude Code, internal codenames, analytics events, and Statsig:

## Tengu: Claude Code's Internal Codename

Tengu appears to be Claude Code's internal control-plane codename: the namespace Anthropic uses for telemetry, experiments, rollout gates, entitlement checks, operational tuning, and internal model overrides.

## Analytics Events and Feature Flags

Telemetry events use the tengu_ prefix (e.g., tengu_update_check, tengu_mcp_start, tengu_oauth_success), and Anthropic uses GrowthBook to gate Claude Code features behind "tengu_"-prefixed flags that can be toggled remotely.

## Relationship with Statsig

Anthropic originally used Statsig for analytics — until OpenAI acquired Statsig in September 2025, which presumably made continuing that arrangement uncomfortable. Anthropic switched to GrowthBook after OpenAI acquired their previous analytics provider, Statsig, in September 2025.

## Control Plane Architecture

Tengu looks like the internal umbrella namespace for Claude Code's product-control plane: analytics, experiments, rollout gates, and operational knobs all live under the same naming scheme. Rather than just a telemetry prefix, the codebase uses tengu_* names for critical functionality including feature gates, permission controls, and operational settings.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Claude Code's migration from Statsig to GrowthBook for feature flags and telemetry:

## Migration Context

Anthropic switched to GrowthBook after OpenAI acquired their previous analytics provider, Statsig, in September 2025. GrowthBook is an open-source feature flagging and A/B testing platform that Anthropic uses to gate Claude Code features behind "tengu_"-prefixed flags that can be toggled remotely.

## Feature Flag Delivery System

A remote settings endpoint on Anthropic's servers is polled every 60 minutes by every running instance of Claude Code, returning a configuration payload that can override local settings and activate or deactivate features. GrowthBook feature flags (prefixed tengu_) gate individual behaviors at a granular level.

## Migration Tools

GrowthBook has created resources to assist with the migration: When working with Claude Code, you can invoke the agent when you need to migrate Statsig code by saying "I need to migrate this code from the Statsig SDK to the GrowthBook SDK: [filename]"

## Issues with Feature Flag Decoupling

A significant concern emerged from the migration: Setting DISABLE_TELEMETRY=1 (or DO_NOT_TRACK) puts Claude Code into a "no-telemetry" traffic mode that disables GrowthBook entirely. GrowthBook isn't only experiment-exposure logging — it's also the delivery mechanism for feature gates. This coupling has caused users who disable telemetry to lose access to paid features that depend on feature flags.

I found relevant search results about GrowthBook feature flags in Claude Code. Here's what the search revealed:

## Claude Code GrowthBook Feature Flags

The terms `isGrowthBookEnabled`, `hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures`, and `growthBookFeaturesLoaded` are diagnostic values used by Claude Code to report the status of its GrowthBook feature flag service.

**Key Information:**

- `growthBookFeaturesLoaded` indicates the number of feature flags that have been successfully fetched from GrowthBook
- `isGrowthBookEnabled()` returns false when the `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK` environment variable is set or when a baseline gate is disabled
- When GrowthBook is disabled, all read APIs degrade to the caller's default value

**Troubleshooting Context:**

In a failing state, `isGrowthBookEnabled=true hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures=false growthBookFeaturesLoaded=0` indicates the GrowthBook service is enabled but feature flags are not loading. GrowthBook is the delivery mechanism for feature flags that gate preview features (like Agent View) and remote killswitches.

These flags are particularly important in Claude Code diagnostics since they control access to experimental features and remote control functionality.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# Issue Analysis: `tengu_ccr_bridge` Flag

## What Feature Does It Gate?

The `tengu_ccr_bridge` flag gates the **Remote Control (`/remote-control`)** feature. CCR stands for **"Claude Code Remote"** (inferred from context).

From the code path shown:
```javascript
function isBridgeEnabled() {
  return getFeatureFlag("tengu_ccr_bridge", false);
}

function isRemoteControlEnabled() {
  return isBridgeEnabled() && isPolicyAllowed("allow_remote_sessions");
}
```

The `/remote-control` command visibility is **entirely gated on `isBridgeEnabled()`**, with a default of `false`.

## What Causes the Flag to Flip False?

The issue identifies **Hypothesis B as most probable**:

> **GrowthBook Cohort Assignment Keyed on Session/Device Identifier** — Fresh login produces new session context → new GrowthBook evaluation → `true`. Background refresh uses stale/different session context → `false`.

**Specific behavior:**
1. **On login**: `tengu_ccr_bridge: true` is cached in `~/.claude.json`
2. **After background refresh**: The flag flips to `tengu_ccr_bridge: false` in `cachedGrowthBookFeatures`
3. **Failure modes**:
   - `/remote-control` → "Unknown skill: remote-control"
   - `claude remote-control` → "Remote Control is not yet enabled for your account"

Supporting evidence: The same account shows `tengu_ccr_bridge: true` on Ubuntu but `false` on macOS (issue #28583), indicating the cohort assignment is device/session-keyed rather than account UUID-keyed.

## Diagnostic Fields Mentioned

No diagnostic fields like `growthBookLastFetched`, `hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures`, or `isGrowthBookEnabled` are explicitly mentioned in the issue. The only relevant diagnostic output shown is:

```json
"hasAvailableSubscription": false,
"oauthAccount": {
  "hasExtraUsageEnabled": true,
  "billingType": "stripe_subscription"
}
```

However, the issue clarifies that `hasAvailableSubscription: false` is a **red herring** — RC works immediately post-login despite this field being false.

Based on the search results, I can provide you with comprehensive information about CCR and related concepts:

## What does CCR stand for?

CCR stands for Claude Code Router.

## What is CCR?

CCR is an open-source proxy that intercepts your Claude Code requests before they reach any provider, then redirects each request to the backend model that makes the most sense for that task. More specifically, Claude Code Router (CCR) is an open-source proxy/middleware tool that acts as a compatibility layer between Claude Code CLI and various AI backend providers.

## How does CCR work?

When you start CCR, it intercepts all outbound requests from your terminal before they reach the default Anthropic endpoint. For each request, it inspects the request, applies your routing rules, transforms the payload into the target provider's expected format, forwards it, and the response comes back the same way, transformed back into the format Claude Code expects, so the client never knows anything changed.

## Key Benefits and Features

CCR solves several problems: vendor lock-in by allowing switching between AI providers without changing workflow, cost optimization by routing tasks to cheaper or free models when appropriate, privacy by enabling use of local models for sensitive code, flexibility by supporting multiple providers simultaneously with intelligent routing, and experimentation by facilitating A/B testing of different models.

CCR eliminates vendor dependency by letting you define routing rules that automatically switch models based on task type, token count, or any custom logic you write.

## Connection to "ccr_bridge" and Remote Control

The Bridge Communication Protocol is the bidirectional messaging layer that connects a local Claude Code CLI instance to the claude.ai server, enabling remote control sessions where a user on claude.ai drives a Claude Code worker running on a developer's machine. This system handles environment registration, work distribution, real-time message streaming, permission delegation, and session lifecycle management across two distinct transport generations (v1 WebSocket and v2 SSE+CCR).


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.prompt: What does CCR stand for in this document about Claude Code's bridge communication protocol? Describe what the CCR transport is, how tengu_ccr_bridge relates, and any expansion of the acronym CCR given.

Based on the search results, I found information about Claude Code's Remote Control feature and the CCR acronym:

## CCR Acronym

CCR is part of Claude Code's v2 transport generation (v2 SSE+CCR), used in the Bridge Communication Protocol. The CCR protocol uses asymmetric transport: reads flow through a persistent connection (WebSocket or SSE), writes go through HTTP POST.

## Remote Control and Feature Flag

Remote Control is gated by a server-side GrowthBook feature flag (tengu_ccr_bridge, defaults to false). The term "tengu" appears in the feature flag name related to the CCR bridge infrastructure.

## What Remote Control Is

The Bridge Communication Protocol is the bidirectional messaging layer that connects a local Claude Code CLI instance to the claude.ai server, enabling remote control sessions where a user on claude.ai drives a Claude Code worker running on a developer's machine.

It's a synchronization layer, not a cloud migration. Your session keeps running on your machine the entire time.

The search results show that "CCR" is used in Claude Code's technical infrastructure for the v2 transport protocol, and the "tengu_ccr_bridge" feature flag controls access to Remote Control functionality, but the exact full-form expansion of the "CCR" acronym itself is not explicitly stated in these results.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.prompt: Does this chapter expand the acronym CCR (as in tengu_ccr_bridge, the Remote Control bridge)? What does it say CCR stands for, and what is the CCR bridge architecture? Also note anything about GrowthBook flags gating remote control.

content.text:
Research complete. Here is the report, with confirmed facts separated from inference.

## Confirmed facts

**1. GrowthBook diagnostic fields (`isGrowthBookEnabled`, `hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures`, `growthBookFeaturesLoaded`, `growthBookLastFetched`, `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK`)**

Claude Code uses GrowthBook (the open-source feature-flag/AB-testing platform) as its remote feature-flag service. The CLI polls a GrowthBook endpoint roughly hourly and caches flag values (in `~/.claude.json` under `cachedGrowthBookFeatures`). The diagnostic items report the health of that subsystem:

- `isGrowthBookEnabled` — whether the GrowthBook client is active at all; returns false when the `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK` env var is set (or a baseline gate is off), in which case all flag reads fall back to hardcoded defaults.
- `growthBookFeaturesLoaded` — count of flags successfully fetched.
- `hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures` / `growthBookLastFetched` — cache freshness; a failing state looks like `isGrowthBookEnabled=true hasFreshGrowthBookFeatures=false growthBookFeaturesLoaded=0`.
- `DISABLE_GROWTHBOOK` is the explicit "no flag traffic, accept frozen defaults" escape hatch. Separately, `DISABLE_TELEMETRY`, `DO_NOT_TRACK`, and `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC` also suppress GrowthBook — a documented complaint, since that silently disables flag-gated features (Agent View, Remote Control, channels) and remote killswitches, not just analytics ([issue #58383](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/58383), [issue #45918](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/45918), [zenn.dev writeup](https://zenn.dev/m0370/articles/d7e77adebd0ba8?locale=en), [gist on the v2.1.76 fix](https://gist.github.com/ahundt/158d498f07a10fec4955a70bf475a20b)).

**2. "tengu" = Claude Code's internal codename**

Well documented from the npm source-map leak and minified-source analyses: `tengu_` is the namespace for Claude Code's entire product control plane — analytics events (`tengu_update_check`, `tengu_oauth_success`, `tengu_mcp_start`), feature gates, killswitches, and operational knobs ([claude-code-decoded TENGU.md](https://github.com/PeronGH/claude-code-decoded/blob/main/TENGU.md), [The Register on the source leak](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/01/claude_code_source_leak_privacy_nightmare/), [hidden-features writeup](https://github.com/sanbuphy/claude-code-source-code/blob/main/docs/en/02-hidden-features-and-codenames.md)).

**3. `tengu_ccr_bridge`**

Confirmed: it is the server-side GrowthBook flag (default `false`) gating **Remote Control** (`/remote-control`) — the bridge that lets claude.ai web/mobile drive a local Claude Code instance. The decompiled logic is essentially `isBridgeEnabled() = getFeatureFlag("tengu_ccr_bridge", false)`, AND-ed with an `allow_remote_sessions` policy check ([issue #34267](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/34267), [#29812](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/29812), [#37140](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/37140), [official docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/remote-control)). A companion flag `tengu_ccr_bridge_multi_session` existed; per the Claude Code Changelog tracker, both were removed around v2.1.66, with `tengu_ccr_bridge` described as "enforces a minimum Claude Code version for Remote Control bridge usage" ([@ClaudeCodeLog](https://x.com/ClaudeCodeLog/status/2029021978288361697)). Known failure modes: the flag flipping false after background GrowthBook cache refresh (fixed by logout/login), and disappearing entirely when telemetry env vars suppress GrowthBook.

**Crucially: it is NOT related to musistudio/claude-code-router.** That community proxy coincidentally shares the "CCR" abbreviation, but `tengu_ccr_bridge` is Anthropic-internal and gates Remote Control. In source analyses, "CCR" names the v2 remote-control transport generation ("v1 WebSocket and v2 SSE+CCR" — asymmetric transport: reads over SSE/WebSocket, writes over HTTP POST) ([zread.ai bridge protocol doc](https://zread.ai/instructkr/claude-code/22-bridge-communication-protocol), [claude-code-from-source ch16](https://claude-code-from-source.com/ch16-remote/)).

**4. Statsig → GrowthBook migration**

Confirmed. Claude Code originally used Statsig for analytics/feature gates; OpenAI acquired Statsig in September 2025, and Anthropic subsequently migrated Claude Code to GrowthBook ([decodethefuture.org](https://decodethefuture.org/en/claude-code-undercover-mode-killswitches-telemetry/), [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/01/claude_code_source_leak_privacy_nightmare/)). Amusingly, GrowthBook itself publishes a Claude Code subagent for migrating Statsig SDK code to GrowthBook ([growthbook/claude-statsig-to-growthbook-sdk-migrator](https://github.com/growthbook/claude-statsig-to-growthbook-sdk-migrator)).

## Speculation / unconfirmed

- **Exact expansion of "CCR"**: no authoritative source spells it out. Community inferences include "Claude Code Remote" (most common, from GitHub issue context) and one search summary's "Claude Cloud Runtime"; the source analyses just treat CCR as the name of the remote-control bridge transport. Anthropic has not published the expansion.
- The claim that the flag flip is caused by device/session-keyed GrowthBook cohort assignment (issue #34267's "Hypothesis B") is user diagnosis, not Anthropic-confirmed.
- The motive for the Statsig→GrowthBook switch (OpenAI acquisition discomfort) is widely repeated but is inference; Anthropic hasn't stated a reason.

Sources: [GH #34267](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/34267) · [GH #29812](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/29812) · [GH #37140](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/37140) · [GH #58383](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/58383) · [GH #45918](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/45918) · [GH #64151](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/64151) · [ClaudeCodeLog tweet](https://x.com/ClaudeCodeLog/status/2029021978288361697) · [zenn.dev](https://zenn.dev/m0370/articles/d7e77adebd0ba8?locale=en) · [ahundt gist](https://gist.github.com/ahundt/158d498f07a10fec4955a70bf475a20b) · [claude-code-decoded TENGU.md](https://github.com/PeronGH/claude-code-decoded/blob/main/TENGU.md) · [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/01/claude_code_source_leak_privacy_nightmare/) · [decodethefuture.org](https://decodethefuture.org/en/claude-code-undercover-mode-killswitches-telemetry/) · [zread.ai bridge protocol](https://zread.ai/instructkr/claude-code/22-bridge-communication-protocol) · [claude-code-from-source ch16](https://claude-code-from-source.com/ch16-remote/) · [growthbook migrator](https://github.com/growthbook/claude-statsig-to-growthbook-sdk-migrator) · [official Remote Control docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/remote-control)
